# B21: Key Quotes & Strategic Questions

## Key Quotes

**"Zoe's fucking awesome. It's like the actual best tool for any non technical person I've ever encountered."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Demonstrating Zoe as an example of AI that controls an actual computer, not just generates text. Illustrating how fitness data could be integrated with learning profiles to create a more personalized, compound intelligence.

**"When I tell it to do something it doesn't just send it to a large language model and return a answer like an auto generated text. It will actually then proceed to take steps to operate that online computer to take out the actions that you want."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Explaining the fundamental shift in AI mental model from conversation to action-taking. Signals that the future of AI is about orchestration and execution, not just information retrieval.

**"I am of the belief that doing it internally is better."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Taking a strong position on AI product development strategy. Rejecting pure outsourcing in favor of internal accountability and control.

**"You need at least one really strong technical leader and product mind on your side who's technical enough to guide, who's technical enough to know when the developers are bullshitting and can like get realistic, can set and demand realistic things from them."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Defining the critical role of internal technical leadership. Emphasizes that this person needs both technical competence and the authority to challenge external developers.

**"A developer company can just throw up their hands and be like, sorry, like we just did what you told us to do."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Highlighting the accountability risk of outsourcing entirely. External teams can deliver what was asked for rather than what's actually needed.

**"You don't need like, yeah, like, you don't need your own model, you don't need fine tuning, you don't need any of that."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Challenging the assumption that AI products require custom model training or fine-tuning. Suggests most use cases are better served with existing foundation models.

**"Large language models are good enough."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Reinforcing that for many AI product applications, especially at early stages, off-the-shelf models are sufficient and avoid unnecessary complexity and cost.

**"My advice was essentially get at least a technical product hybrid in house. Even if you do everything out of house, having one responsible person in house that can like, manage and corral everyone is vital."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Summarizing the core strategic recommendation: even if you outsource development, you need internal coordination and accountability.

**"The tools are accessible enough without, you know, hand waving too much about the, the complexities and everything."**  
— Logan Currie  
*Context:* Noting the democratization of AI tooling—you don't need deep ML expertise to build valuable AI products anymore.

**"There's a lot that's possible now with many of these tools that even six months ago would not have been possible."**  
— Logan Currie  
*Context:* Highlighting the rapid pace of AI advancement. What's possible has fundamentally shifted in a very short timeframe, reducing barriers to entry.

**"Basically every like 8 out of \$10 or 80 cents out of each dollar went to salaries basically."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Providing transparent insight into Careerspan's cost structure. Dominant cost is people, not infrastructure or AI compute.

**"Plan for [unit economics]... keep an eye on the unit economics from the outset."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Warning against a common startup failure mode: prioritizing growth and experience, then discovering the business model doesn't work at scale.

**"We made a big mistake with our first co founder because we wanted him to... build a product, right? That's what you fucking expect when you get a technical co founder."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Sharing a painful learning about technical hiring. Leadership credentials don't equal building capability, especially for early-stage work.

**"Sometimes the folks that have been in director roles for a long time are actually farthest away from the tech."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Context:* Revealing a counterintuitive truth about technical seniority. Management experience can distance people from hands-on technical skills needed in early stages.

## Strategic Questions Raised

**"What's the AI strat? Like, what is the thinking at ACE, around where y'all want to invest those AI dollars or AI R&D dollars?"**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Significance:* This question probed beneath the surface of "we're exploring AI" to understand strategic intent and priorities. It revealed ACE is thinking about AI as a study coach/mentor with just-in-time content and scenario-based learning.

**"What do folks say when it comes to like just out of curiosity because I, I actually I've been getting into fitment fitness more recently... one of the things that has been helpful to me was not just its ability to give advice or even present the right resource but specifically for it to reliably and securely track my other data and centralize it in a way that made it actionable."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Significance:* This reframed the conversation from content delivery to data integration and personalization. Introduced the idea of compounding learner profiles by bringing in external data sources (Fitbit, 23andMe, etc.).

**"What is that?"**  
— Merisenda Alatorre  
*Significance:* Asking what Careerspan learned about AI development approaches—internal vs external, hiring, technical leadership. This opened a substantive discussion about mistakes made, lessons learned, and practical advice for ACE's AI journey.

**"If you do have something like a JD, happy to scan against our pool of candidates for them."**  
— Vrijen Attawar  
*Significance:* Offers to help with technical hiring using Careerspan's candidate pool, but the question implied was: "Do you have job descriptions? Can we help?" Demonstrates collaborative value exchange.

**"Did you outsource the, like, AI engineering or building?"**  
— Merisenda Alatorre  
*Significance:* Direct question about Careerspan's build vs buy strategy, which elicited the key insight that AI engineering was done in-house by Ilsa (who served as both CTO and Head of AI).

**"I don't know if you're able to share this or even if you could just give a range of like ballpark what were your development costs both or over time?"**  
— Merisenda Alatorre  
*Significance:* Courageous direct question about financials that elicited valuable transparency about cost structure (80% salaries) and the importance of monitoring unit economics from the start.
